Juttadinteria suavissima is a small, clumping succulent with very soft, velvety grey-green leaves. It forms low mats and produces daisy-like flowers in the growing season. Native to arid southern Africa, it likes bright light, sharp drainage and infrequent watering.

Mammillaria unknown is a small, globular cactus with rounded tubercles instead of ribs. It has dense spines, often forms clumps, and blooms a ring of small colorful flowers. Easy to grow with bright light, good drainage, and infrequent watering.

Mammillaria unknown is a small, round cactus covered with nipple-like bumps (tubercles) and short spines. It often forms clumps and produces a ring of small, colorful flowers in spring or summer. Easy to grow: bright light, minimal water, well-draining soil.

Opuntia species (prickly pear) are flat-padded cacti with spines and tiny barbed glochids, producing bright flowers and sweet, edible fruits. They store water, thrive in dry, sunny places, and are used for food, gardens, and erosion control.

Opuntia (prickly pear) is a cactus with flat, paddle-like pads covered in spines and tiny barbed glochids. It produces bright flowers and edible fruits. Tough and drought-tolerant, it prefers sunny, well-drained soil and thrives in dry climates.

Raphianacme flanaganii is a tuberous, drought‑tolerant succulent from southern Africa. It forms a swollen underground caudex and sends up thin seasonal stems with tiny leaves. In season it produces clusters of small pale tubular flowers and is grown as a caudiciform plant.

Sedum pachyphyllum is a low-growing succulent with thick, blue-green leaves that blush red in strong sun. It forms trailing clumps and produces small yellow star-shaped flowers in summer. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow in pots, rock gardens, or sunny borders.

Senecio crassisimus is a compact succulent with upright stems and thick, pale blue-green leaves that may redden in strong sun. It forms small clusters and produces yellow, daisy-like flowers. Easy-care and drought-tolerant, it suits sunny, well-drained pots and rock gardens.

Unknown Gasteria is a small, slow-growing succulent with thick, tongue-shaped leaves often mottled with spots or bands. It prefers bright, indirect light, well-draining soil, and infrequent watering. Easy-care and compact, it makes a great indoor or rock-garden plant.

Adenium boehmianum is a striking, drought-tolerant succulent shrub with a swollen, bottle-like trunk (caudex) and smooth grey bark. It bears clusters of vivid pink to deep red tubular flowers and small glossy leaves, thriving in full sun and well-drained soil.

Aloe cameronii is a striking, medium-to-large succulent from southern Africa. It forms rosettes of thick, spiky green to reddish leaves edged with small teeth and produces tall spikes of tubular orange-red flowers—attractive to birds and ideal for hot, dry gardens.

Aloe harlana is a clumping succulent forming rosettes of thick, toothed green leaves. It sends up tall spikes of tubular orange-red flowers that attract birds and insects. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow in bright light and well-draining soil.

Aloe jacksonii is a small to medium succulent forming rosettes of thick, triangular leaves edged with tiny teeth. In season it produces tall flower spikes with tubular orange-red blooms that attract birds and insects. Easy to grow in sunny, well-drained conditions.

Aloe jucunda is a small, clumping succulent forming rosettes of thick, toothed green leaves often with pale spots. It produces tall stalks of tubular orange-red flowers. Hardy and drought-tolerant, it’s well suited to rock gardens, containers, or dry landscapes.

Aloe maculata is a hardy, spotted-leaf succulent with broad, thick, green to gray-green leaves and vibrant orange to red tubular flowers on tall stalks. It tolerates drought, attracts pollinators, and is often used in gardens and landscapes.

Aloe munchii is a small succulent forming rosettes of thick, toothed green leaves. In season it sends up tall stalks of tubular orange-red flowers that attract birds. It likes full sun, well-drained soil and tolerates drought.

Aloe rivae is a small, clumping succulent from East Africa with thick, triangular green leaves often marked with pale spots. It sends up tall stalks of tubular orange-red flowers. Drought-tolerant and sun-loving, it’s ideal for rock gardens and containers.

Aloe rivierei is a compact succulent forming dense rosettes of thick, triangular leaves with toothed margins. In summer it sends up tall stalks of tubular orange–red flowers that attract pollinators. Drought-tolerant and ideal for rock gardens or containers.

Dracaena serpenta is a compact tropical houseplant with narrow, often patterned strap-like leaves growing from cane-like stems. It tolerates low light, prefers moderate watering and well-draining soil, and is a popular, easy-care choice for homes and offices.

Dracaena (unknown species) is a tropical houseplant with upright stems and glossy, sword-like leaves. Easy to grow, it tolerates low light and occasional watering. Commonly kept indoors for its air-purifying qualities and neat, architectural appearance.

Euphorbia tirucalli, called pencil cactus or milk bush, is a succulent with smooth, pencil-like green stems and few leaves. It stores water, tolerates drought, and is often grown as an ornamental. Its milky sap is toxic and can irritate skin and eyes.

Gasteria obliqua is a small South African succulent with thick, triangular, dark-green leaves often marked with pale spots. Leaves form a fan-like arrangement. It produces short stems of pink, tubular flowers and grows slowly in bright, indirect light.

Gasteria is a small, slow-growing succulent from South Africa with thick, fleshy, mottled leaves arranged in rosettes or two-ranked rows. It prefers bright, indirect light, infrequent watering, and well-draining soil. Easy to grow and ideal for pots and indoor gardens.

Haworthia sp. are small, low-growing succulent rosettes with thick, often patterned or translucent leaves. Easy to care for, they prefer bright light, infrequent watering, and good drainage. Ideal for pots, windowsills, and beginner gardeners.

Tephrocactus articulatus is a small, jointed cactus with round, segmented stems and distinctive papery spines. It forms clumps of detachable joints that root easily. Produces white to pink flowers; thrives in bright sun, well-drained soil and sparse watering.

Stetsonia coryne is a tall, tree-like cactus from the Gran Chaco of South America. It forms thick columnar stems with dark spines, bears large white night-blooming flowers and red edible fruits, and thrives in hot, dry scrubland as a dramatic ornamental.

Carnegiea gigantea, the saguaro cactus, is a giant columnar cactus of the Sonoran Desert. It grows slowly for centuries, developing upward arms, ribbed flesh and sharp spines. Saguaros store water, bloom fragrant white flowers, and provide food and shelter for wildlife.

Yucca elata, the soaptree yucca, is a desert shrub with stiff, sword-shaped leaves and a tall flower stalk of hanging white bell-shaped blooms. Tough and drought-tolerant, it thrives in sandy soils and adds sculptural form to arid landscapes.

Brachychiton rupestris, the Queensland bottle tree, is a small to medium Australian tree with a swollen, water-storing trunk, glossy green lobed leaves, and clusters of creamy bell-shaped flowers. Very drought-tolerant, it’s popular as an ornamental and street tree.

Brachychiton species are Australian trees or shrubs known for thick, often bottle-shaped trunks, variable lobed leaves and clusters of bell-like cream, pink or red flowers. They store water, tolerate drought, and make low-maintenance ornamental trees.

Hohenbergia magnispina is a striking bromeliad with a rosette of stiff, heavily spined leaves and a tall central flower spike bearing colorful tubular blooms. It prefers warm, humid conditions and makes a dramatic, architectural plant for gardens or containers.

Aloe deltoideodonta is a small, rosette-forming succulent with triangular, toothed leaves and spiky margins. It sends tall flower stalks with tubular orange or red blooms that attract birds and bees. Drought-tolerant, it prefers full sun and well-draining soil.

Calibanus hookeri is a slow-growing, caudiciform succulent from northeastern Mexico. It forms a stout, woody trunk (caudex) topped by dense tufts of narrow, grasslike gray-green leaves and produces small clusters of white-green flowers. Very drought-tolerant and great in containers.

Cephalocereus senilis, the "old man cactus," is a slow-growing columnar cactus covered in long white hairs that look like a beard. Native to Mexico, it likes bright light, warmth, little water, and well-draining soil. Protect it from frost.

Copiapoa gigantea is a slow-growing, large clumping cactus from the arid coastal deserts of northern Chile. It forms thick, ribbed blue-green stems with stout spines and woolly crowns, producing yellow flowers. It tolerates intense sun and very little water.

Dracaena ballyi 'Tom Grumbley' is a compact, slow-growing succulent houseplant with short, upright mottled leaves and subtle banding. Tough and low-maintenance, it tolerates low light and infrequent watering, making it perfect for small pots, desks, or beginner plant owners.

Agave 'Dragon's Toes' is a compact succulent forming a tight rosette of finger-like, variegated leaves with green centers and bright yellow margins. Tough and drought-tolerant, it’s perfect for containers or rock gardens in full sun to light shade.

Aloe arborescens 'Variegata' is a compact, evergreen succulent with spiky, green leaves edged in creamy-yellow variegation. It forms clumping rosettes, produces bright red-orange tubular flowers in winter, tolerates drought and sun, and suits containers or dry gardens.

Dracaena pethera is a popular, easy-care indoor plant with upright cane-like stems and glossy, variegated leaves edged in cream or yellow. It tolerates low light, prefers moderate watering, and adds a graceful, tropical look to homes and offices.

Dracaena trifasciata 'Moonshine' is a slow-growing snake plant with silvery-green, upright swordlike leaves. It's low-maintenance, tolerates low light and drought, and makes a great indoor air-purifying houseplant. Keep soil well-draining and water sparingly.

Gasteria batesiana 'Variegata' is a small, slow-growing succulent with thick, cream-and-green striped leaves arranged in compact rosettes. Easy-care and drought-tolerant, it prefers bright, indirect light, well-draining soil, and infrequent watering—great for pots or rock gardens.

Nopalea cochenillifera forma variegata is a small, paddle-shaped cactus with cream-and-green variegated, often nearly spineless pads. It bears bright magenta tubular flowers in summer, tolerates drought, and is an attractive, low-maintenance ornamental for sunny, well-drained spots.

Ferocactus glaucescens, the blue barrel cactus, is a round, slow-growing cactus with powdery blue-green skin and strong hooked golden spines. It produces bright yellow flowers and red fruits, tolerates sun and drought, and suits rock gardens or containers.

Pilosocereus palmeri is a tall, columnar cactus from Mexico with bluish-green ribbed stems covered in woolly areoles and sharp spines. It produces night-blooming, pale tubular flowers and small fleshy fruits. Often grown as an ornamental and provides shelter for wildlife.

Opuntia echios is a prickly-pear cactus native to the Galápagos Islands. It forms shrubby to tree-like clumps of flat, water-storing pads with sharp spines, bright yellow flowers and red edible fruits. It thrives on dry volcanic soils and provides food and shelter for wildlife.

Euphorbia buruana is a small succulent shrub native to Buru Island in Indonesia. It has green, water-storing stems, tiny clustered flowers (called cyathia) and milky sap. It prefers rocky or open sites and its latex can irritate skin if handled.

Aloe acutissima var. antanimorensis is a small, drought-tolerant succulent forming spiky rosettes of thick, toothed leaves. It sends up tall stalks of tubular orange-red flowers in season. Easy to grow in full sun with well-draining soil or in containers.

Aloe conifera is a small succulent from Madagascar with rosettes of thick, triangular green leaves often edged with tiny teeth. It bears tall spikes of tubular orange-red flowers that attract pollinators and thrives in sunny, well-drained soil.

Aloe erythrophylla is a small, clumping succulent with fleshy, often reddish leaves arranged in a rosette. It produces tall spikes of tubular orange-red flowers. Drought-tolerant and sun-loving, it thrives in well-draining soil and suits rock gardens or containers.

Aloe rauhii is a small, clumping aloe from Madagascar with thick, blue-green leaves edged in reddish teeth. It makes short spikes of tubular orange-red flowers and grows slowly in rock gardens or pots, preferring bright light and good drainage.

Didieria madagascariensis is a spiny, drought‑tolerant shrub or small tree native to Madagascar’s dry spiny forests. It has thick succulent stems with clusters of small leaves and sharp thorns, and bears modest pale flowers and fruits.

Euphorbia aff croizatii is a small, cactus-like succulent with ribbed or cylindrical spiny stems and tiny inconspicuous flowers. It produces a milky, irritating sap. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow in bright light with well-draining soil; handle with care.

Euphorbia betrokana is a small, spiny succulent shrub from Madagascar with thick, water-storing stems and seasonal small leaves. It produces milky, toxic sap and clusters of tiny cup-like flowers called cyathia. Drought-tolerant and adapted to rocky, dry habitats.

Euphorbia capsaintemariensis is a small, low-growing succulent from southern Madagascar. It forms clumps of fleshy, blue-green stems, bears tiny cup-like flowers (cyathia), and stores water for drought. Its milky sap is irritating, so handle with care.

Euphorbia croizatii is a small succulent spurge native to Madagascar. It forms compact, fleshy, branching stems with tiny leaves and small, simple flowers. It exudes milky sap, tolerates dry, rocky habitats, and is popular with succulent collectors.

Euphorbia decaryi is a low-growing, mat-forming succulent from Madagascar. It has creeping, cylindrical bluish-green stems and small cup-shaped yellow to red flowers. Drought-tolerant and sun-loving, it’s popular in rock gardens and containers for its compact, attractive habit.

Euphorbia decaryi var. spirosticha is a low, creeping succulent from Madagascar that forms mats of twisted, pencil-like stems. It has tiny leaves and small, colorful cyathia (flower-like blooms). Drought-tolerant and compact, it suits rock gardens or containers. Handle with care; sap can irritate skin.

Euphorbia duranii is a small, clump-forming succulent with fleshy, cylindrical green stems. It prefers bright light, sharp drainage and infrequent watering. It produces tiny flowers and contains a milky, toxic sap—handle with care. Great for rock gardens or cactus collections.

Euphorbia genoudiana is a small, compact succulent spurge native to Madagascar. It forms fleshy stems or clumps, often with tiny leaves, and bears small yellow-green cyathia (flower-like structures). It contains milky sap, so handle with care.

Euphorbia hedyotoides is a small succulent spurge with fleshy, cylindrical stems and often tiny or no leaves. It produces small, yellowish cyathia (flower-like structures), prefers bright light and well-draining soil, is drought-tolerant, and has toxic milky sap—handle with care.

Euphorbia millii var. roseana, a compact, thorny succulent shrub, bears clusters of rose‑colored bracts around tiny flowers. Drought‑tolerant and sun‑loving, it makes a popular indoor or patio plant. Sap is milky and poisonous—avoid skin or eye contact.

Euphorbia platyclada is a low, leafless succulent from Madagascar with flat, sprawling greenish-brown stems that look like twigs. It grows close to the ground, bears tiny cyathia (flowers), stores water in its stems, and has milky sap that can irritate skin.

Euphorbia platyclada var. hardyi is a low-growing, leafless succulent with flattened, ribbon-like green stems that sprawl across the ground. It makes tiny flower clusters (cyathia) and contains a milky, irritating sap—handle carefully.

Kalanchoe beauverdii is a small, easy-care succulent with thick, scalloped leaves and upright stems that bear clusters of tubular, bell-like flowers. It prefers bright light, well-drained soil and infrequent watering, making it a hardy choice for pots or sunny windowsills.

Kalanchoe beharensis is a slow-growing succulent from Madagascar with large, velvety, triangular scalloped leaves that are brownish-green and fuzzy underneath. It forms an architectural, branched shrub, prefers bright light and well-draining soil, and tolerates periods of drought.

Kalanchoe bracteata is a compact, easy-care succulent with glossy, fleshy leaves and upright stems that bear small clustered tubular flowers. It prefers bright light, well-draining soil and infrequent watering, making it ideal for pots, windowsills, and beginner gardeners.

Kalanchoe dinklagei is a small succulent forming low clumps of thick, scalloped-edged leaves. It’s drought-tolerant, prefers bright light and well-draining soil, and sends up slender stalks of small tubular flowers. Easy to grow as a houseplant or in rock gardens.

Kalanchoe orgyalis is a small, easy-care succulent with glossy brown-to-copper paddle-shaped leaves and a paler underside. It forms compact shrubs and produces clusters of tubular yellow flowers in winter. Prefers bright light, well-draining soil, and infrequent watering.

Kalanchoe rhombopilosa is a small, slow-growing Madagascar succulent with thick, rhomboid gray-green leaves fringed by brown scalloped edges. It forms low clumps and tiny tubular flowers. Easy-care: bright light, well-draining soil, and infrequent watering.

Kalanchoe viguieri is a succulent shrub from Madagascar with thick, fleshy leaves and woody stems. It forms upright clumps and produces clusters of small tubular flowers. Easy-care and drought-tolerant, it prefers bright light and well-draining soil.

A small, drought-tolerant succulent from Madagascar, Kalanchoe viguieri var. angustisepala forms compact rosettes of fleshy leaves and produces clusters of narrow-sepaled tubular flowers. Easy-care, prefers bright light and well-draining soil; good for pots and rock gardens.

Kleinia madagascariensis is a drought-tolerant succulent from Madagascar. It forms fleshy, often-branching stems with simple leaves and produces small yellow, daisy-like flowers. Easy to grow in bright light and well-drained soil, ideal for pots or rock gardens.

Senecio descoingsii is a low-growing succulent from southern Africa. It forms tight clumps of fleshy, blue-green leaves and produces small yellow, daisy-like flowers. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow, it prefers bright light and well-draining soil.

Senna meridionalis is a small leguminous shrub with pinnate (compound) leaves, bright yellow pea-like flowers in clusters and flat seed pods. It blooms in warm months, attracts bees and butterflies, and is often grown for ornament and habitat enhancement.

Agave 'Blue Glow' is a compact, slow-growing succulent with a tight rosette of blue-green leaves edged in creamy to pink margins. Drought-tolerant and low-maintenance, it thrives in sunny, well-drained spots or containers, making a striking architectural focal point.

Agave albopilosa is a small, slow-growing Mexican succulent forming compact rosettes of soft, gray-green leaves covered in fine white hairs. Leaves have tiny marginal teeth and a sharp tip. It flowers once on a tall spike, then the rosette dies, often producing pups.

Agave americana subsp. americana, the century plant, is a large, slow-growing succulent with rosettes of thick blue-green, spiny leaves. It sends up a dramatic tall flower spike once before dying. Extremely drought-tolerant, it’s popular in dry gardens and landscapes.

Agave angustifolia is a narrow-leaved agave forming a compact rosette of blue-green, tooth-edged leaves tipped with a sharp spine. Drought-tolerant and sun-loving, it produces a tall flowering stalk once in its life. Often grown as an ornamental and used in mezcal production.

Agave attenuata, or foxtail agave, is a spineless succulent with a rosette of soft, arching pale-green leaves. Slow-growing and drought-tolerant, it sends up a dramatic curved flower spike and thrives in containers or sunny, well-drained garden spots.

Agave attenuata variegata is a striking, spineless agave with soft, green leaves edged in creamy yellow. It forms graceful rosettes and clumps of offsets, producing tall flower spikes. Drought-tolerant and low-maintenance, it thrives in sun or light shade.

Agave bovicornuta 'Reggae Time' is a compact, rosette-forming succulent with blue-green, fleshy leaves edged by reddish, cow‑horn-like teeth. It makes a striking architectural accent, tolerates heat and drought, prefers full sun and well-draining soil, and needs minimal water.

Agave bracteosa is a small, spineless agave from Mexico forming low rosettes of smooth, flexible, curling blue-green leaves. It sends up a tall flower stalk with pale green blooms, produces offsets, and is prized for drought-tolerant, low-maintenance gardens.

Agave mitis is a small, clumping succulent from Mexico with low, neat rosettes of soft blue-gray, spoon-shaped leaves and tiny marginal teeth. Slow-growing and drought-tolerant, it sends up a tall flower stalk once, then the main rosette dies leaving offsets.

Agave polianthiflora is a small, clumping succulent with slender, blue-gray, gently twisted leaves edged with tiny teeth. Native to Mexico, it’s drought-tolerant and slow-growing, sending up a tall stalk of many pale-green tubular flowers before the rosette dies.

Agave potatorum, the butterfly agave, forms a compact rosette of blue-gray, spoon-shaped leaves with toothed margins and a sharp tip. Slow-growing and drought-tolerant, it suits containers and rock gardens, sending up a tall flowering stalk once before the main rosette declines.

Agave potatorum 'Kissho Kan' is a compact, slow-growing dwarf agave forming a tight rosette of thick, scalloped leaves with small marginal teeth. Ideal for containers or rock gardens, it's drought-tolerant, prefers sun and very well-drained soil, and needs little care.

Agave tequilana, or blue agave, is a Mexican succulent with thick, spiny blue-green leaves in a tight rosette. It flowers once after several years; its sweet core is harvested to make tequila. Drought-tolerant and widely grown commercially.

Agave titanota is a compact, slow-growing succulent forming a striking rosette of thick, blue-gray leaves edged with large teeth and a sharp tip. It stays small (usually under 3 feet), loves full sun, well-drained soil, and is drought-tolerant.

Agave victoriae-reginae is a small, slow-growing succulent from Mexico that forms a tight rosette of thick green leaves marked with white lines and ending in sharp tips. Drought-tolerant and compact, it’s ideal for rock gardens, containers, and dry landscapes.

Astrophytum myriostigma, called bishop's cap, is a spineless, star-shaped cactus from Mexico. It has ribbed green stems with white speckles and produces yellow, daisy-like flowers. Slow-growing and drought-tolerant, it prefers bright light and well-draining soil.

Astrophytum ornatum is a slow-growing, column-shaped cactus with pronounced ribs and white speckling on dark green skin. It produces bright yellow flowers and thrives in bright light, warm temperatures, and well-draining soil. Popular and drought-tolerant for cactus collections.

Beaucarnea recurvata, the ponytail palm, is a slow-growing, drought-tolerant houseplant with a swollen, water-storing trunk and long, arching straplike leaves. It likes bright light, minimal watering, and makes an easy, sculptural addition to homes or patios.

Echeveria carnicolor is a compact, rosette-forming succulent with thick, powdery leaves that blend green, cream and pink tones. It produces small flower stalks and offsets. Easy to grow, it prefers bright light, well-draining soil and infrequent watering.

Echinocereus brandegeei is a small clumping hedgehog cactus with cylindrical, ribbed stems and sharp spines. It produces showy pink to magenta spring flowers. Native to rocky, dry places, it tolerates heat, sun, and infrequent watering—ideal for pots or rock gardens.

Echinocereus pentalophus is a small, clumping cactus with slender, five-ribbed stems that arch or trail. It produces bright pink to magenta, funnel-shaped flowers in spring. Easy-care, drought-tolerant, it prefers full sun and well-draining soil.

Euphorbia lomelii is a drought-tolerant succulent shrub from Mexico with thick, cylindrical green stems that form dense clumps. It bears small bright red‑orange, slipper‑shaped flowers and has a milky sap that can irritate skin—good for dry rock gardens.

Ferocactus hystrix is a barrel-shaped desert cactus with thick ribbed stems and strong hooked spines. It forms solitary or clustered clumps and produces colorful funnel-shaped flowers and small fruits. Adapted to hot, dry habitats, it prefers full sun and minimal water.

Ferocactus pilosus is a stout, barrel-shaped cactus with thick ribs, woolly areoles and strong, often curved spines. It produces bright, funnel-shaped flowers and small fruits. Native to Mexico, it tolerates heat and drought and suits rock gardens.

Ferocactus rectispinus is a barrel-shaped cactus with stout, straight spines giving a bold, architectural look. Native to arid areas, it grows slowly into solitary or clumping mounds and produces showy flowers and small fruit. Durable and drought-tolerant.

Graptopetalum amethystinum is a low-growing succulent with thick, plump, powdery purple-lavender leaves in neat rosettes. It forms spreading clumps and bears small star-shaped pale pink flowers. Easy-care: needs bright light, well-draining soil and occasional watering.

Graptopetalum paraguayense, or ghost plant, is a low-growing, trailing succulent with pale blue-gray to pinkish rosettes of thick, fleshy leaves. It forms mats and offsets, blooms small star-shaped white-pink flowers, and is drought-tolerant and easy to grow in bright light.

Hamatocactus hamatacanthus subsp. hamatacanthus is a small, slow-growing globular cactus with stout, often hooked spines. It produces showy yellow to orange funnel-shaped flowers and prefers sunny, well-drained, dry soils. Native to arid parts of Mexico, it suits rock gardens or containers.

Leuchtenbergia principis is a striking, slow-growing cactus from Mexico that forms a rosette of long triangular tubercles ending in papery, pointed tips. It produces yellow flowers and prefers bright sun, warm dry conditions and sharp, well-draining soil, popular with collectors.

Lophocereus schottii, the senita cactus, is a slow-growing columnar cactus with blue-green, ribbed stems and short spines. It bears nocturnal white flowers and sweet fruits, thrives in hot dry deserts, and is pollinated by a specialized senita moth.

Mammillaria decipiens is a small clumping cactus that forms low mats of rounded stems covered in tubercles and short spines. In spring it produces a ring of pink to white flowers, followed by tiny red fruits. Prefers bright light and well drained soil.

Mammillaria geminispina is a small, clustering cactus with rounded stems covered in dense white radial spines and darker hooked central spines. It bears bright pink to magenta funnel-shaped flowers and prefers strong light, well-draining soil, and warm, dry conditions.

Mammillaria hahniana 'Super Clone' is a small, clumping cactus with dense white wool and silvery spines. It produces bright pink, funnel-shaped flowers in spring. Easy to grow, it prefers bright light, well-draining soil and infrequent watering.

Mammillaria parkinsonii is a small clumping cactus native to central Mexico. Rounded stems are covered in tubercles and spines. In spring it makes a ring of pink to magenta funnel-shaped flowers, followed by small red fruits. Prefers bright light and well-drained soil.

Mammillaria petterssonii is a small, clumping cactus with rounded stems covered in soft tubercles and short radial spines. It produces rings of small pink to magenta flowers. Easy to grow, it prefers bright light and well‑draining, dry soil.

Mammillaria vetula is a small, clumping cactus from Mexico that forms cushions of round green stems covered with tiny tubercles and short spines. It produces small pink or lavender flowers and red fruits, and grows well in bright, dry conditions.

Lophocereus marginatus, the Mexican fencepost cactus, is a tall, columnar cactus with blue-green, ribbed stems and short spines. It’s drought-tolerant, grows into upright columns or hedges, prefers full sun and well-draining soil—great for low-care gardens.

Opuntia aciculata is a low-growing prickly pear cactus with flat oval pads covered in dense fine barbed spines (glochids). It bears bright yellow to orange flowers and red fruits. Drought-tolerant and sun-loving, it prefers well-drained soil.

Opuntia robusta is a hardy prickly pear cactus native to Mexico. It forms shrubby, tree-like clumps of large rounded blue-green pads covered in tiny spines and glochids. It bears bright pink flowers and edible red fruit.

Lophocereus schottii f. monstrosus is a slow-growing, monstrose form of the senita cactus. It has thick, twisted, brain-like columnar stems with irregular bumps and few spines. Hardy and sculptural, it makes a striking specimen in dry gardens or containers.

Pachycereus are large columnar cacti native to Mexico and the southwestern United States. They grow tall, tree-like stems with thick ribs, produce white night-blooming flowers and edible fruits, and tolerate extreme drought, supporting desert wildlife and landscape uses.

Succulent Pachyphytum bracteosum forms small rosettes of plump, powdery blue-green leaves that slowly cluster into mounds. In season it sends tall stalks of pink to red bell-shaped flowers. Easy to grow, it prefers bright light and well-draining soil.

Sedum adolphii, called golden sedum, is a low-growing succulent with fleshy golden-green leaves that blush red in strong sun. It forms trailing rosettes and small yellow star-shaped flowers. Easy-care and drought-tolerant, ideal for containers, rock gardens, and sunny spots.

Sedum morganianum, called burro’s tail or donkey tail, is a trailing succulent with plump blue-green teardrop leaves that spill from hanging baskets. It’s drought-tolerant, likes bright light and well-draining soil, and bears small pink star-shaped flowers.

Cleistocactus hyalacanthus is a tall, slender columnar cactus with many thin stems covered in short golden or whitish spines. It bears narrow, tubular red-orange flowers that stay mostly closed, attracting hummingbirds and adding color to rock or cactus gardens.

Fouquieria splendens, commonly called ocotillo, is a spiny desert shrub with tall, whip-like stems that leaf out after rain and bloom bright red tubular flowers along the stems. Native to southwestern North American deserts, it attracts hummingbirds and tolerates heat and poor soils.

Ariocarpus fissuratus is a slow-growing, spineless cactus that looks like a low, gray-green button on the ground. Native to dry, rocky deserts of Texas and Mexico, it has triangular, warty tubercles and displays pink to magenta flowers in autumn.

Adenia spinosa is a small, thorny caudiciform shrub from southern Africa with a swollen water-storing trunk, spiny stems and seasonal lobed leaves. Drought-tolerant and slow-growing, it makes an unusual, sculptural container plant prized by collectors.

Aloe alooides is a small, easy-care succulent with rosettes of thick, blue-green leaves edged with tiny teeth. It sends up tall stalks of tubular orange-red flowers that attract pollinators. Prefers sunny, well-drained spots and tolerates drought.

Aloe arborescens is a spreading, shrub-like succulent with thick, spiny, gray-green leaves in rosettes. In winter it sends up tall stalks of bright red-orange tubular flowers that attract birds and pollinators. Drought-tolerant and widely used in gardens and traditional medicine.

Aloe broomii is a small to medium spiky aloe forming a rosette of thick, fleshy leaves edged with teeth. In spring it sends tall stalks of orange-red tubular flowers that attract birds. Very drought-tolerant and easy to grow.

Aloe ferox is a large, stemless succulent from South Africa with thick, spiky, grey-green leaves and tall stalks of bright orange-red tubular flowers. Drought-tolerant and hardy, it’s used in gardens and traditional medicine for skin and digestive remedies.

Aloe krapohliana is a compact, drought-tolerant succulent forming tight rosettes of thick, blue-green leaves edged with small teeth. In season it produces tall stems of tubular orange-red flowers. Easy to grow, it suits rock gardens, containers and dry landscapes.

Aloiampelos ciliaris is a vine-like succulent with long, spiky green leaves fringed by tiny hairs. It climbs or scrambles and produces tall spikes of bright red-orange tubular flowers that attract birds. Native to South Africa, it’s hardy and drought-tolerant.

Boophane disticha is a South African bulbous plant with flat, two-ranked leaves and a tall leafless stalk bearing a cluster of showy pink to salmon funnel-shaped flowers. It handles dry, rocky soils and has a poisonous bulb that was used traditionally as arrow poison.

Bowiea volubilis, the climbing onion, is a bulbous succulent with a round underground bulb and thin, twining green stems. Native to Africa, it tolerates drought, prefers bright light and well-draining soil, and bears tiny flowers; all parts are mildly toxic.

Crassula arborescens, called the silver dollar or jade plant, is a small woody succulent with thick round bluish-green leaves that often turn red at the edges in sun. It forms compact shrubs with pale pink star-shaped flowers. Easy-care, drought-tolerant; prefers bright light and well-draining soil.

Crassula ovata, commonly called the jade plant, is a popular succulent with thick, glossy oval leaves and woody stems. It stores water, prefers bright light and infrequent watering, and makes a low-maintenance indoor or outdoor ornamental that can live many years.

Crassula ovata 'Hobbit' is a compact succulent with thick woody stems and chubby tubular leaves curled at the tips like little horns. Easy-care and slow-growing, it prefers bright light, well-draining soil, and infrequent watering, and may produce small pink flowers.

Cyphostemma juttae is a drought-tolerant, caudiciform succulent from Namibia and South Africa. It forms a dramatic swollen, bottle-shaped trunk topped by a crown of pinnate leaves, bears small clustered flowers and grape-like fruit, and is grown as a striking ornamental or bonsai.

Euphorbia horrida 'Snowflake' is a spiny, cactus-like succulent with dark green, ribbed stems dusted with white speckles that resemble snow. Compact and drought-tolerant, it forms columnar clumps with sharp spines and small flowers; milky sap may irritate skin.

Euphorbia obesa, the "baseball plant", is a round, slow-growing succulent from South Africa. It forms green, ribbed balls with faint stripes and small summer flowers. Easy to grow in bright light and well-draining soil; its milky sap is toxic—avoid contact.

Gasteria excelsa is a South African succulent with thick, straplike leaves covered in rough bumps or white spots. It forms clumps, grows slowly, and sends up tall stalks of tubular pinkish flowers. Easy care: bright, indirect light and well-draining soil.

Haemanthus albiflos is a bulbous evergreen from South Africa with broad, glossy strap-shaped leaves. In winter or spring it produces round clusters of white, brush-like flowers with yellow stamens. Easy-care, it tolerates shade and dry conditions, great for pots or gardens.

Lithops geyeri is a tiny “living stone” succulent with paired, round fleshy leaves patterned in gray-green and brown spots. Native to arid southern Africa, it produces yellow daisy-like flowers and needs bright light, excellent drainage, and very little water.

Lithops hookeri is a tiny "living stone" succulent with two thick, patterned leaves that mimic pebbles to avoid grazing. It grows slowly, forms solitary or clustered heads, flowers with daisy-like blooms, and thrives in bright light with very little water.

Pachypodium saundersii is a spiny, succulent shrub from southern Africa with a thick water-storing trunk and narrow, glossy leaves. It bears clusters of fragrant, tubular white to pink flowers and tolerates drought, making it a popular, low-maintenance ornamental for sunny, well-drained spots.

Curio ficoides is a low-growing succulent with fleshy, spoon-shaped blue-green leaves that form tight clumps. It produces short stems and occasional small white flowers. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow, it prefers bright light and well-draining soil—ideal for pots or rock gardens.

Kleinia stapeliiformis is a low-maintenance succulent with pencil-like, jointed green stems that look like cacti. It forms trailing clumps perfect for pots or hanging baskets, tolerates drought and bright sun, and produces small orange-yellow daisy-like flowers.

Strelitzia juncea, the rush-leaved bird-of-paradise, is an evergreen South African perennial with narrow, cylindrical leaves and dramatic orange-and-blue crane-like flowers. It forms a clumping shrub to about 2–3 m, tolerates drought and sun, and is a popular ornamental.

Tylecodon paniculatus is a slow-growing succulent shrub from southern Africa. It has thick, gray-green leaves on stout stems and produces showy clusters of tubular yellow to orange flowers in panicles. It prefers sunny, well-drained, rocky sites and tolerates drought.

Welwitschia mirabilis is a strange, ancient desert plant from the Namib Desert. It grows only two long, ribbon-like leaves that split and fray, a short woody stem, and deep roots. It can live many centuries and survives on fog and scarce rain.

Begonia ruhlandiana is a compact, shade-loving perennial begonia with asymmetrical, textured leaves and clusters of small delicate flowers. It prefers bright, indirect light, consistently moist but well-draining soil, and warm, humid conditions, making it an attractive indoor or shaded garden plant.

Bromelia balansae is a large, ground-forming bromeliad from South America with stiff, spiny-edged leaves arranged in a rosette. It produces a tall central flower spike with bright red-orange bracts and tubular blooms, then often makes pups after flowering.

Cleistocactus species are slender, upright cacti with many ribs and spines. They form tall columnar stems and produce tubular, often brightly colored flowers that sometimes stay partly closed. Easy to grow in bright light and well-draining soil, they make striking container plants.

Copiapoa coquimbana is a slow-growing, globular to short columnar cactus from Chile’s coastal Atacama near Coquimbo. It forms clumps of gray-green stems with short spines and yellow flowers. Drought-tolerant and fog-adapted, it prefers rocky, well-draining soil.

Copiapoa hypogaea is a small, often partly buried cactus from northern Chile. It forms low, rounded clumps with dark green to gray bodies, few or no spines, and produces bright yellow flowers in season. It survives in hot, very dry coastal deserts.

Copiapoa lembeckei is a slow-growing, clumping cactus from Chile’s coastal deserts. It forms rounded, gray-green stems with short spines, tolerates extreme drought, and produces striking yellow to orange, cup-shaped flowers. Easy to grow in well-draining soil.

Dyckia sp. are hardy, terrestrial bromeliads with stiff, spiny rosettes of succulent-like leaves and silver-green tones. They form tall flower spikes of yellow-orange blooms, prefer sunny, rocky, well-drained sites, and tolerate drought and poor soils.

Echinopsis huascha is a small, round cactus from Argentina that forms solitary or small clumps. Covered in short spines, it produces striking trumpet-shaped red to orange flowers that open briefly in spring–summer. Prefers sunny, well-drained rocky or sandy soil.

Echinopsis species are round or columnar cacti with ribs and spines that produce spectacular, large funnel-shaped flowers in white, pink, red or yellow. They’re easy to grow in bright light with fast-draining soil and moderate, infrequent watering.

Gymnocalycium saglionis is a globose to short cylindrical cactus from Argentina. It forms large, blue‑green bodies with pronounced ribs and stout radial spines. In spring it bears big white to pale pink funnel-shaped flowers and tolerates sun and dry conditions.

Parodia magnifica is a small, round cactus with smooth blue-green ribs and woolly areoles. It stays compact, produces bright yellow, funnel-shaped flowers in summer, and prefers bright light, fast-draining soil, and infrequent watering—an easy, decorative houseplant.

Oreocereus trollii is a woolly columnar cactus from the high Andes (Bolivia/Peru). Covered in dense white hairs and long spines, it protects against cold and sun. It forms upright stems and bright pink to red tubular flowers near the top in summer.

Pilosocereus sp. is a columnar, tree-like cactus with blue-green stems, pronounced ribs and clusters of spines. It produces night-blooming white or yellow flowers and fruit. Drought-tolerant and heat-loving, it is popular as an ornamental in sunny, well-drained locations.

Uebelmannia pectinifera is a small, slow-growing cactus from Brazil (Minas Gerais). It forms rounded to short columnar stems with rough, ribbed tubercles and comb-like spines. In spring it produces small yellow flowers and is found on sunny, rocky outcrops.

Aloe macrocarpa is an East African succulent forming compact rosettes of thick, spotted gray-green leaves. It sends up tall stalks of tubular red-orange flowers. Drought-tolerant and easy to grow, it prefers full sun and well-draining soil.

Aloidendron dichotomum, the quiver tree, is a tall succulent native to southern Africa. It has thick, branching trunks with rosettes of spiky blue-green leaves and bright yellow flowers. Very drought-tolerant, it grows on rocky slopes with a striking silhouette.

Crassula 'Tom Thumb' is a tiny, low-growing succulent with thick, glossy, paired rounded leaves that form dense mats. It has short stems and small white or pink-tinged flowers. Easy-care and drought-tolerant, it’s great for rock gardens, containers, or mixed succulent displays.

Dracaena hallii is a compact, slow-growing succulent with short, thick, upright leaves often mottled dark green and silvery gray. It forms tight clumps, tolerates low light and drought, and makes an easy, low-maintenance houseplant for pots and small spaces.

Fockea edulis is a slow-growing succulent native to southern Africa with a large, round water-storing tuber and thin twining stems. It bears small, sweet-scented, pale flowers and seasonal leaves. Drought-tolerant and ornamental, it’s prized for its sculptural caudex in pots.

Haworthiopsis limifolia is a small, easy-care succulent with tight rosettes of thick, ridged triangular leaves. Native to South Africa, it forms clumps of dark green to brownish foliage, prefers bright light, fast-draining soil, and infrequent watering—great for pots or windowsills.

Parodia leninghausii is a small columnar cactus with soft golden spines that form fuzzy, clustered stems. It produces bright yellow, daisy-like flowers in summer. Native to Brazil, it prefers bright light, fast-draining soil, and occasional watering.

Hemionitis bradburyi is a small evergreen fern with shiny, arrowhead-shaped fronds arising from a short creeping rhizome. It thrives in moist, shaded spots on soil or rocks and is valued for its bold, simple leaves in gardens and terrariums.

Pelargonium carnosum is a small, fleshy-leaved geranium native to South Africa. It forms low, succulent stems with water-storing leaves and produces clusters of delicate flowers. Suitable for sunny, well-drained spots; drought-tolerant and easy to care for.

Yucca baccata, the banana yucca, is a low-growing evergreen with rosettes of stiff, swordlike leaves and tall stalks of creamy white bell-shaped flowers. Native to the southwestern US, it produces banana-shaped edible fruits and thrives on dry, rocky slopes.

Leucophyllum frutescens, commonly called Texas sage or barometer bush, is a drought-tolerant evergreen shrub with silvery-gray leaves and showy purple-pink tubular flowers that often bloom after rain. It stays compact, needs little care, and attracts bees and hummingbirds.

Agave lechuguilla, a small, spiky desert agave native to the Chihuahuan Desert, forms dense rosettes of stiff, toothed leaves. It sends up a tall flowering stalk once, then dies. Very drought-tolerant and an important plant in arid landscapes.

Cylindropuntia (cholla) is a group of spiny desert cacti with cylindrical stem segments covered in sharp, often barbed spines. They produce colorful flowers and fleshy fruits, survive hot dry climates, and easily break apart to spread and root nearby.

Larrea tridentata, the creosote bush, is an evergreen desert shrub of the American Southwest and northern Mexico. It has small yellow flowers, a strong resinous scent after rain, deep roots, extreme drought tolerance, and long-lived clonal growth.

Opuntia phaecantha is a small prickly pear cactus with flattened green pads, sharp spines and tiny hair-like glochids. It bears bright yellow to orange flowers in spring and red edible fruits, thriving in sunny, dry, rocky soils of the Southwest.

Opuntia santa-rita 'Tubac' is a hardy prickly pear cactus with flat, spiny pads that often blush reddish-purple in cool weather. It produces bright yellow flowers and tasty red fruits. Drought-tolerant and sun-loving, it's ideal for low‑maintenance dry gardens.

Simmondsia chinensis, commonly called jojoba, is a drought-tolerant evergreen shrub from southwestern North America. It has leathery leaves and produces oval seeds that yield liquid wax (jojoba oil), widely used in skin and hair care products for gentle moisturizing.

Yucca faxoniana is a tree-like yucca with a single stout trunk topped by a dense rosette of stiff, blue-gray, sword-shaped leaves with sharp tips. It produces tall stalks of creamy-white, bell-shaped flowers and thrives in dry, rocky desert climates.

Opuntia basilaris, the beavertail cactus, is a low-growing desert plant with smooth, flat paddle-like pads and tiny barbed hairs (glochids). In spring it bears showy magenta flowers with yellow centers and thrives in dry, rocky soils of the southwestern United States.

Opuntia basilaris hyb. 'Baby Rita' is a compact, low-growing beavertail cactus with smooth, flattened pads and bright magenta-pink flowers. Drought-tolerant and sun-loving, it’s ideal for rock gardens, containers, and water-wise landscapes.

Agave parrasana is a small, compact succulent forming tight rosettes of thick blue-gray leaves edged with soft teeth and a sharp tip. Slow-growing and drought-tolerant, it makes striking architectural accents and, like other agaves, flowers once then dies after seeding.

Chilopsis linearis, the desert willow, is a small, drought-tolerant tree with narrow willow-like leaves and long bean-like seed pods. It bears showy pink to purple trumpet flowers in summer that attract hummingbirds and bees, making it popular for xeriscapes in arid regions.

Euphorbia antisyphilitica, called candelilla, is a leafless, upright succulent shrub from the Chihuahuan Desert. It has narrow waxy green stems, tiny yellow flowers and a milky sap. Extremely drought-tolerant, it’s valued for candelilla wax and dry-land landscaping.

Euphorbia milii, called crown-of-thorns, is a small succulent shrub with woody, thorny stems and bright red, pink, or yellow petal-like bracts. It tolerates heat and drought, blooms most of the year in warm spots, and has milky, irritating sap.
